                "headline": "Special Weather Statement issued January 18 at 6:48PM PST by NWS Pendleton OR",
                "description": "Areas of freezing fog of less than a quarter of a mile will\ncontinue to linger along parts of I-84 near Cabbage Hill, Poverty\nFlats, and Deadman Pass. Road cameras confirm obscure visibility\nin the area. Practice extra pre-caution while driving, such as\nleaving more space in front of you when traveling.                 "headline": "Cold Weather Advisory issued January 18 at 8:47PM CST until January 19 at 12:00PM CST by NWS Quad Cities IA IL",
                "description": "* WHAT...Very cold wind chills as low as 20 to 30 below expected.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of north central and northwest Illinois and east\ncentral and southeast Iowa.\n\n* WHEN...From 3 AM to noon CST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The dangerously cold wind chills could cause frostbite\non exposed skin in 30 minutes or less. Frostbite and hypothermia\nwill occur if unprotected skin is exposed to these temperatures.",
                "instruction": "Use caution while traveling outside. Wear appropriate clothing, a\nhat, and gloves.\n\nKeep pets indoors as much as possible.\n\nMake frequent checks on older family, friends, and neighbors. Ensure\nportable heaters are used correctly. Do not use generators or grills\ninside.",

                "senderName": "NWS Bismarck ND",
                "headline": "Cold Weather Advisory issued January 18 at 8:43PM CST until January 19 at 10:00AM CST by NWS Bismarck ND",
                "description": "* WHAT...Very cold wind chills as low as 35 below expected.\n\n* WHERE...Northwest and much of central North Dakota, including the\nJames River Valley.\n\n* WHEN...Until 10 AM CST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The cold wind chills as low as 35 below could cause\nfrostbite on exposed skin in as little as 10 minutes.",
                "instruction": "Limit time outdoors, and make sure to dress in layers and cover\nexposed skin if outside.",
                "response": "Prepare",

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Grand Rapids MI",
                "headline": "Heavy Freezing Spray Warning issued January 18 at 9:42PM EST until January 20 at 7:00AM EST by NWS Grand Rapids MI",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Gale Warning, west winds increasing to 35 knot\ngales and waves 8 to 13 feet. For the Heavy Freezing Spray\nWarning, heavy freezing spray at a rate of up to one inch per\nhour expected, and may rapidly accumulate on vessels.\n\n* WHERE...St Joseph to Manistee.\n\n* WHEN...For the Gale Warning, until 7 PM EST Monday. For the\nHeavy Freezing Spray Warning, from 1 AM Monday to 7 AM EST\nTuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in heavy freezing spray is\nhazardous. Freezing spray may render mechanical and electronic\ncomponents inoperative. Rapid ice accretion on decks and\nsuperstructures may result in a catastrophic loss of\nstability. Strong winds will cause hazardous waves which could\ncapsize or damage vessels and reduce visibility.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for dangerous accumulation of ice on\ntheir vessel. If possible, remain in port, avoid the warning area\nor conduct mitigation.\n\nMariners should alter plans to avoid these hazardous conditions.\nRemain in port, seek safe harbor, alter course, and/or secure the\nvessel for hazardous conditions.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {

                "headline": "Cold Weather Advisory issued January 18 at 8:39PM CST until January 19 at 12:00PM CST by NWS Chicago IL",
                "description": "* WHAT...Very cold wind chills as low as 20 below to 25 below zero\nexpected.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of central, east central, north central, and\nnortheast Illinois.\n\n* WHEN...From 3 AM to noon CST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The dangerously cold wind chills as low as 25 below zero\ncould cause frostbite on exposed skin in as little as 30 minutes.",
                "instruction": "Use caution while traveling outside. Wear appropriate clothing, a\nhat, and gloves.",

                "description": "* WHAT...Snow showers and blowing snow. Winds gusting as high as 45\nmph. Reduced visibility in falling and blowing snow.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.\n\n* WHEN...Until midnight CST tonight.\n\n* IMPACTS...Plan on slippery road conditions.                 "event": "Special Weather Statement",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Quad Cities IA IL",
                "headline": "Special Weather Statement issued January 18 at 8:30PM CST by NWS Quad Cities IA IL",
                "description": "Strong northwest winds are expected tonight behind an arctic cold\nfront. Gusts up to 45 mph are anticipated through about midnight,\nwith isolated higher gusts possible over portions of eastern\nIowa. It will stay windy through the night with gusts between 30\nto 40 mph continuing into early Monday morning. Scattered snow\nshowers may accompany the strong winds, resulting in sudden and\nlocalized visibility reductions to under 1 mile. Additionally,\nblowing and drifting snow is possible where there is currently\nshallow snow cover, mainly north of Interstate 80. Bitterly cold\nconditions are expected tonight with wind chills quickly dropping\ninto the teens and 20s below zero.",
                "instruction": null,
                "response": "Execute",

                "headline": "Cold Weather Advisory issued January 18 at 9:28PM EST until January 20 at 9:00AM EST by NWS Indianapolis IN",
                "description": "* WHAT...Very cold wind chills as low as 17 below zero expected.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of central, east central, north central, and west\ncentral Indiana.\n\n* WHEN...From 4 AM Monday to 9 AM EST Tuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The dangerously cold wind chills as low as 20 below zero\ncould result in hypothermia or frostbite if precautions are not\ntaken. Very cold temperatures can lead to hypothermia with\nprolonged exposure.",
                "instruction": "Use caution while traveling outside. Wear appropriate clothing, a\nhat, and gloves.\n\nKeep pets indoors as much as possible.\n\nMake frequent checks on older family, friends, and neighbors. Ensure\nportable heaters are used correctly.                 "description": "* WHAT...Lake effect snow and blowing snow expected. Total snow\naccumulations of 2 to 6 inches, with the highest amount in the far\nnortheastern part of the county.                 "description": "* WHAT...For the Wind Advisory, southwest winds 15 to 25 mph with\ngusts up to 45 mph expected. For the Cold Weather Advisory, very\ncold wind chills as low as 15 below expected.\n\n* WHERE...In Ohio, Ashtabula Inland, Ashtabula Lakeshore, Cuyahoga,\nand Lake Counties. In Pennsylvania, Northern Erie and Southern\nErie Counties.\n\n* WHEN...For the Wind Advisory, from 10 AM to 7 PM EST Monday. For\nthe Cold Weather Advisory, from 7 PM Monday to noon EST Tuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Gusty winds will blow around unsecured objects. Tree\nlimbs could be blown down and a few power outages may result. The\ncold wind chills as low as 15 below zero could result in\nhypothermia if precautions are not taken.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...The strongest winds and gusts are expected\nfrom the lakeshore to Interstate 90.",

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Cleveland OH",
                "headline": "Cold Weather Advisory issued January 18 at 9:04PM EST until January 20 at 12:00PM EST by NWS Cleveland OH",
                "description": "* WHAT...Very cold wind chills as low as 15 below expected.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of north central and northeast Ohio and northwest\nPennsylvania.\n\n* WHEN...From 7 PM Monday to noon EST Tuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The cold wind chills as low as 15 below zero could\nresult in hypothermia if precautions are not taken.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...The strongest winds and gusts are expected\nfrom the lakeshore to Interstate 90.",
                "instruction": "Use caution while traveling outside. Wear appropriate clothing, a\nhat, and gloves.",
                "response": "Prepare",
                "parameters": {

                "headline": "Freeze Warning issued January 18 at 8:58PM EST until January 19 at 9:00AM EST by NWS Tallahassee FL",
                "description": "* WHAT...Sub-freezing temperatures as low as 22 expected.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of southeast Alabama and south central and\nsouthwest Georgia.\n\n* WHEN...From 4 AM EST /3 AM CST/ to 9 AM EST /8 AM CST/ Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Frost and freeze conditions could kill crops, other\nsensitive vegetation and possibly damage unprotected outdoor\nplumbing.",
                "instruction": "Take steps now to protect tender plants from the cold.",

                "event": "Special Weather Statement",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Raleigh NC",
                "headline": "Special Weather Statement issued January 18 at 8:55PM EST by NWS Raleigh NC",
                "description": "Roads remain wet from earlier precipitation. As temperatures fall\ninto the mid to upper 20s, patchy black ice will be possible\novernight and into early Monday morning. Secondary, less traveled\nroads, bridges, overpasses, and sidewalks will be especially\nsusceptible.\n\nBlack ice is difficult to see and distinguish from dry surfaces.\nThe deceptive nature of black ice can catch you unaware while you\nare driving or walking, causing you to lose control of your motor\nvehicle or to slip and fall.",
                "instruction": "If you plan to drive or walk, remain alert for black ice. Slow\nyour driving speed and use care when walking on sidewalks,\ndriveways or parking lots. Slips and falls constitute a leading\ncause of injuries during winter weather.",
                "response": "Execute",

                "event": "High Surf Advisory",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Honolulu HI",
                "headline": "High Surf Advisory issued January 18 at 3:26PM HST until January 19 at 6:00AM HST by NWS Honolulu HI",
                "description": "...HIGH SURF ADVISORY FOR NORTH AND WEST FACING SHORES OF NIIHAU\nKAUAI AND OAHU...\n...HIGH SURF ADVISORY FOR NORTH FACING SHORES OF MOLOKAI AND\nMAUI...\n\n.A moderate to large, long period northwest swell (320 degrees)\nwill bring advisory level surf to exposed north and west facing\nshores tonight.\n\n* WHAT...Surf heights rising to 15 to 20 feet for north and west\nfacing shores of Niihau and Kauai, north facing shores of Oahu,\nMolokai and Maui. 12 to 16 feet along west facing shores of\nNiihua and Kauai. 8 to 12 feet along west facing shores of\nOahu.\n\n* WHERE...North and west facing shores of Niihau, Kauai and Oahu,\nand north facing shores of Molokai and Maui.\n\n* WHEN...Until 6 AM HST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Moderate. Strong breaking waves and strong currents\nwill make swimming dangerous.",
                "instruction": "Heed all advice from ocean safety officials. When in doubt, don't\ngo out.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "CFWHFO"
                    ],

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Newport/Morehead City NC",
                "headline": "Special Weather Statement issued January 18 at 8:24PM EST by NWS Newport/Morehead City NC",
                "description": "Minimal drying of Sunday's rainfall and below freezing temps will\ncreate patchy black ice on area roads, bridges, overpasses,\nparking lots, sidewalks, driveways, and other paved surfaces.\n\nThese thin sheets of ice may be difficult to see, especially\nduring the pre-dawn hours. They may appear to be wet spots on the\nroad. Motorists will have a difficult time determining which spots\nare just wet and which are icy.",
                "instruction": "If you plan to drive or walk, remain alert for black ice. Slow\nyour driving speed and use care when walking on sidewalks,\ndriveways or parking lots. Slips and falls constitute a leading\ncause of injuries during winter weather.",
                "response": "Execute",

                "event": "Special Weather Statement",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Wakefield VA",
                "headline": "Special Weather Statement issued January 18 at 8:24PM EST by NWS Wakefield VA",
                "description": "With temperatures dropping well below freezing tonight into Monday\nmorning, black ice development is likely as wet roads freeze.\nRoads may therefore become slick and slippery, especially on\nuntreated, secondary, and back roads.\n\nSlow down and use caution when driving, especially when traversing\nroads that have not been treated. If you are walking outside,\nwatch your first few steps taken on stairs, sidewalks, and\ndriveways. These surfaces could be icy and slippery, increasing\nyour risk of a fall and injury.\n\nRoad and sidewalk conditions should improve later Monday morning\nas temperatures warm to above freezing.",
                "instruction": null,
                "response": "Execute",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "SPSAKQ"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [

                    ],

                        "AREAS OF BLACK ICE ACROSS TONIGHT INTO MONDAY MORNING"
                    ],

                    ],

                "description": "* WHAT...For the Dense Fog Advisory, visibility one quarter mile or\nless in dense fog. For the Air Stagnation Advisory, poor air\nquality will continue. This is due to an inversion and stagnant\nair conditions near the surface that will continue to trap\npollutants.\n\n* WHERE...Lower Snake River Plain.\n\n* WHEN...For the Dense Fog Advisory, until noon MST Monday. For the\nAir Stagnation Advisory, until noon MST Tuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Periods of air stagnation can lead to the buildup of\npollutants near the surface. Low visibility could make driving\nconditions hazardous.\n\nIdaho Transportation Department weather stations along Interstates\n84 and 86 indicate visibility does fall to as little as 500 feet,\nmaking normal high way speeds dangerous.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...The fog has moved into the American Falls\narea already, and should spread eastward to the Pocatello area\nduring the evening tonight.",

                "senderName": "NWS Wilmington NC",
                "headline": "Special Weather Statement issued January 18 at 8:14PM EST by NWS Wilmington NC",
                "description": "Lingering water on surfaces from rainfall on Sunday will create\npatchy black ice on area roads into early Monday morning. Icy\nspots could form on paved surfaces such as bridges and overpasses,\nprimary and secondary roads, parking lots, sidewalks and\ndriveways.\n\nBlack ice is difficult to see and distinguish from dry surfaces.\nThe deceptive nature of black ice can catch you unaware while you\nare driving or walking, causing you to lose control of your motor\nvehicle or to slip and fall.",
                "instruction": "If you plan to drive or walk, remain alert for black ice. Slow\nyour driving speed and use care when walking on sidewalks,\ndriveways or parking lots. Slips and falls constitute a leading\ncause of injuries during winter weather.",
                "response": "Execute",
                "parameters": {

                        "SPSILM"
                    ],

                "senderName": "NWS Quad Cities IA IL",
                "headline": "Special Weather Statement issued January 18 at 6:55PM CST by NWS Quad Cities IA IL",
                "description": "Strong northwest winds are expected tonight behind an arctic cold\nfront. Gusts up to 45 mph are anticipated through about midnight,\nwith isolated higher gusts possible over portions of eastern\nIowa. It will stay windy through the night with gusts between 30\nto 40 mph continuing into early Monday morning. Scattered snow\nshowers may accompany the strong winds, resulting in sudden and\nlocalized visibility reductions to under 1 mile. Additionally,\nblowing and drifting snow is possible where there is currently\nshallow snow cover, mainly north of Interstate 80. Bitterly cold\nconditions are expected tonight with wind chills quickly dropping\ninto the teens and 20s below zero.",

                "event": "Rip Current Statement",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS San Juan PR",
                "headline": "Rip Current Statement issued January 18 at 8:37PM AST until January 20 at 6:00AM AST by NWS San Juan PR",
                "description": "* WHAT...Life-threatening rip currents.\n\n* WHERE...Beaches along the northwest to east coasts of the\nislands.\n\n* WHEN...Through late Monday night.\n\n* IMPACTS...Rip currents can sweep even the best swimmers away\nfrom shore into deeper water, where it becomes difficult to\nreturn to safety.",
                "instruction": "There is a high risk of rip currents.\n\nRip currents are powerful channels of water flowing quickly away\nfrom shore, which occur most often at low spots or breaks in the\nsandbar and in the vicinity of structures such as groins, jetties\nand piers. Heed the advice of lifeguards, beach patrol flags and\nsigns.\n\nIf you become caught in a rip current, yell for help. Remain\ncalm, do not exhaust yourself and stay afloat while waiting for\nhelp. If you have to swim out of a rip current, swim parallel to\nshore and back toward the beach when possible. Do not attempt to\nswim directly against a rip current as you will tire quickly.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "CFWSJU"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [
                        "WHCA42 TJSJ 190037"
                    ],
                    "NWSheadline": [
                        "HIGH RIP CURRENT RISK REMAINS IN EFFECT THROUGH LATE MONDAY NIGHT"
                    ],
                    "BLOCKCHANNEL": [

                        "NWEM",
                        "CMAS"

                "senderName": "NWS Baltimore MD/Washington DC",
                "headline": "Cold Weather Advisory issued January 18 at 7:35PM EST until January 20 at 1:00PM EST by NWS Baltimore MD/Washington DC",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Wind Advisory Western Grant and Western Pendleton\nCounties in eastern West Virginia, west winds 20 to 30 mph with\ngusts 45 to 55 mph expected. For the Cold Weather Advisory, very\ncold wind chills as low as 19 below expected.\n\n* WHERE...Western Grant and Western Pendleton Counties.\n\n* WHEN...For the Wind Advisory, from 8 AM to 8 PM EST Monday. For\nthe Cold Weather Advisory, from 8 PM Monday to 1 PM EST Tuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Gusty winds will blow around unsecured objects. Tree\nlimbs could be blown down and a few power outages may result. The\ndangerously cold wind chills as low as 19 below zero could cause\nfrostbite on exposed skin in as little as 30 minutes. Wind chill\nvalues can lead to hypothermia with prolonged exposure.",
                "instruction": "Winds this strong can make driving difficult, especially for high\nprofile vehicles. Use extra caution.\n\nUse caution while traveling outside. Wear appropriate clothing, a\nhat, and gloves.\n\nKeep pets indoors as much as possible.",

                "headline": "Cold Weather Advisory issued January 18 at 5:48PM CST until January 19 at 12:00PM CST by NWS Grand Forks ND",
                "description": "* WHAT...Very cold wind chills as low as 40 below.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of central, north central, northwest, and west\ncentral Minnesota and northeast and southeast North Dakota.\n\n* WHEN...Until noon CST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The dangerously cold wind chills as low as 40 below zero\ncould cause frostbite on exposed skin in as little as 10 minutes.",

                "headline": "Small Craft Advisory issued January 18 at 6:46PM EST until January 19 at 7:00AM EST by NWS Caribou ME",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Small Craft Advisory, north winds 10 to 20 kt\nwith gusts up to 30 kt and seas 3 to 6 ft.                 "description": "* WHAT...Blizzard conditions.                 "description": "* WHAT...For the Dense Fog Advisory, visibility one quarter mile or\nless in dense fog.                 "headline": "Air Stagnation Advisory issued January 18 at 4:14PM MST until January 22 at 11:00AM MST by NWS Boise ID",
                "description": "* WHAT...An extended period of stagnant air, with light winds and\nlittle vertical mixing.                 "instruction": "Persons with respiratory illness should follow their physicians\nadvice for dealing with high levels of air pollution during periods\nof stagnant air.",

                "headline": "Wind Advisory issued January 18 at 6:10PM EST until January 20 at 12:00AM EST by NWS Pittsburgh PA",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Wind Advisory, west winds 15 to 25 mph with gusts up\nto 60 mph expected. For the Cold Weather Advisory, very cold wind\nchills as low as 20 below zero expected.\n\n* WHERE...Eastern Tucker County.\n\n* WHEN...For the Wind Advisory, from 8 AM Monday to midnight EST\nMonday Night. For the Cold Weather Advisory, from 9 PM Monday to\nnoon EST Tuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Gusty winds will blow around unsecured objects.                 "instruction": "Please report downed trees, power lines or large branches by calling\n412-262-1988, posting to the NWS Pittsburgh Facebook page, or using X\n@NWSPittsburgh.\n\nUse caution while traveling outside. Wear appropriate clothing, a\nhat, and gloves.",

                "description": "* WHAT...For the Wind Advisory, west winds 10 to 20 mph with gusts\nup to 50 mph expected. For the Cold Weather Advisory, very cold\nwind chills as low as 21 below expected.\n\n* WHERE...Northwest Pocahontas and Southeast Randolph Counties.\n\n* WHEN...For the Wind Advisory, from 8 AM to 7 PM EST Monday. For\nthe Cold Weather Advisory, from 6 PM Monday to 2 PM EST Tuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Gusty winds will blow around unsecured objects.                 "headline": "Beach Hazards Statement issued January 18 at 3:01PM PST until January 20 at 8:00PM PST by NWS Eureka CA",
                "description": "* WHAT...Increased threat of sneaker waves expected.\n\n* WHERE...Mendocino Coast County.\n\n* WHEN...From Tuesday morning through Tuesday evening. .\n\n* IMPACTS...Large, unexpected waves can sweep across the beach\nwithout warning, sweeping people into the sea from rocks,\njetties, and beaches. These sneaker waves can also move large\nobjects such as logs, crushing anyone caught underneath.",
                "instruction": "Don't be fooled by an ocean that looks calm.  There can be\n30 minutes of small waves before a sneaker wave strikes.  Avoid\nrocks and jetties. Avoid steep beaches.  Stay much farther back\nfrom the water and never turn your back on the ocean.",

                "instruction": "Use caution while traveling outside.                 "description": "* WHAT...For the Freeze Warning, sub-freezing temperatures as low as\n32 expected.                 "instruction": "Use caution while traveling outside. Wear appropriate clothing, a\nhat, and gloves.\n\nKeep pets indoors as much as possible.\n\nMake frequent checks on older family, friends, and neighbors. Ensure\nportable heaters are used correctly.                 "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Pendleton OR",
                "headline": "Air Stagnation Advisory issued January 18 at 1:42PM PST until January 20 at 4:00PM PST by NWS Pendleton OR",
                "description": "* WHAT...Poor air quality will continue. This is due to an inversion\nand stagnant air conditions near the surface that will continue to\ntrap pollutants.\n\n* WHERE...In Oregon, Central Oregon and North Central Oregon. In\nWashington, Kittitas Valley.\n\n* WHEN...Until 4 PM PST Tuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Poor air quality may cause issues for people with\nrespiratory problems.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Area coverage of freezing fog has reduced\nsince this morning, but lingering fog along parts of I-82, I-97,\nand Highway 126 will persist through the next couple of hours.\nExpecting more freezing fog to redevelop overnight tonight for the\nregion.",
                "instruction": "An Air Stagnation Advisory indicates that due to limited movement of\nan air mass across the advisory area, pollution has the potential to\nincrease to dangerous levels. Persons with respiratory illness\nshould follow their physicians advice for dealing with high levels\nof air pollution during periods of stagnant air, state air quality\nagencies highly recommend that no outdoor burning occur and that\nresidential wood burning devices be limited as much as possible.\nAccording to state air quality agencies, prolonged periods of\nstagnant air can hold pollutants close to the ground, where people\nlive and breathe. Check with your local burn agency for any current\nrestrictions in your area.",
                "response": "Execute",
                "parameters": {

                "headline": "Air Stagnation Advisory issued January 18 at 1:42PM PST until January 20 at 4:00PM PST by NWS Pendleton OR",
                "description": "* WHAT...Poor air quality will continue. This is due to an inversion\nand stagnant air conditions near the surface that will continue to\ntrap pollutants.\n\n* WHERE...Foothills of the Blue Mountains of Oregon and Washington,\nEastern Columbia River Gorge of Oregon and Washington, Lower\nColumbia Basin of Oregon and Washington, Yakima Valley, and Simcoe\nHighlands.\n\n* WHEN...Until 4 PM PST Tuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Poor air quality may cause issues for people with\nrespiratory problems.",
                "instruction": "An Air Stagnation Advisory indicates that due to limited movement of\nan air mass across the advisory area, pollution has the potential to\nincrease to dangerous levels. Persons with respiratory illness\nshould follow their physicians advice for dealing with high levels\nof air pollution during periods of stagnant air, state air quality\nagencies highly recommend that no outdoor burning occur and that\nresidential wood burning devices be limited as much as possible.\nAccording to state air quality agencies, prolonged periods of\nstagnant air can hold pollutants close to the ground, where people\nlive and breathe. Check with your local burn agency for any current\nrestrictions in your area.",

                "senderName": "NWS Milwaukee/Sullivan WI",
                "headline": "Flood Warning issued January 18 at 3:41PM CST by NWS Milwaukee/Sullivan WI",
                "description": "...The National Weather Service in Milwaukee/Sullivan WI has issued\na Flood Warning for the following rivers in Wisconsin...\n\nBark River Near Rome affecting Jefferson County.\n\n.Ice build up from the recent cold snap has resulted in the Bark\nRiver near Rome rising into minor flood stage. A Flood Warning is in\neffect until further notice for the Bark River near Rome in\nJefferson County.\n\n* WHAT...Minor flooding is occurring. This approaches the flood of\nrecord.\n\n* WHERE...Bark River near Rome.\n\n* WHEN...From this afternoon until further notice.\n\n* IMPACTS...At 4.3 feet, There is lowland and agricultural land\nflooding.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- At 3:00 PM CST Sunday the stage was 4.2 feet.\n- Bankfull stage is 3.0 feet.\n- Recent Activity...The maximum river stage in the 24 hours\nending at 3:00 PM CST Sunday was 4.3 feet.\n- Forecast...No forecast is available for this location.\n- Flood stage is 4.0 feet.\n- Flood History...This crest compares to a previous crest of\n4.2 feet on 01/21/2019.\n- http://www.weather.gov/safety/flood",
                "instruction": "Turn around, don't drown when encountering flooded roads. Most flood\ndeaths occur in vehicles.\n\nAdditional information is available at www.weather.gov.\n\nThe next statement will be issued late tonight at 345 AM CST.",

                "event": "Small Craft Advisory",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Key West FL",
                "headline": "Small Craft Advisory issued January 18 at 4:29PM EST until January 19 at 5:00AM EST by NWS Key West FL",
                "description": "* WHAT...Northwest to north winds increasing to near 25 knots.\nSeas building to up to 9 feet across the SE Gulf, the western\nStraits of Florida, and western Hawk Channel. Seas building to\nup to 8 feet in the eastern Straits of Florida, eastern Hawk\nChannel, and the nearshore Gulf waters west of the Marquesas\nKeys.\n\n* WHERE...Straits of Florida, Hawk Channel, and Florida Bay and\nthe nearshore Gulf waters.\n\n* WHEN...In Effect.\n\n* IMPACTS...A Small Craft Advisory means that wind speeds of 20\nto 33 knots, or seas of 7 feet or greater, are expected to\nproduce hazardous conditions for small craft. These hazardous\nconditions may result in poor vessel handling and steering\nresponse, broaching, overturned dinghies and kayaks, slips and\nfalls on slippery decks, rub damage along docks and seawalls,\nswamped bows at reef tract mooring balls, and dragging anchors.",
                "instruction": "Inexperienced boaters, especially those with smaller vessels,\nshould avoid operating in these conditions.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "MWWKEY"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [
                        "WHUS72 KKEY 182129"
                    ],

                "event": "Heavy Freezing Spray Warning",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Duluth MN",
                "headline": "Heavy Freezing Spray Warning issued January 18 at 3:16PM CST until January 19 at 9:00AM CST by NWS Duluth MN",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Small Craft Advisory, northwest winds 20 to 30\nkt and waves 5 to 8 ft expected. For the Heavy Freezing Spray\nWarning, heavy freezing spray at a rate of 2 cm per hour or\ngreater expected, and may rapidly accumulate on vessels.\n\n* WHERE...Duluth MN to Port Wing WI, Port Wing to Sand Island\nWI, Sand Island to Bayfield WI and Outer Apostle Islands\nBeyond 5 NM from Mainland.\n\n* WHEN...For the Small Craft Advisory, until midnight CST Monday\nnight. For the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning, from 8 PM this\nevening to 9 AM CST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in heavy freezing spray is\nhazardous. Freezing spray may render mechanical and electronic\ncomponents inoperative. Rapid ice accretion on decks and\nsuperstructures may result in a catastrophic loss of\nstability. Conditions will be hazardous to small craft.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Brief and localized gales up to 35 knots\nwill be possible late this evening and tonight.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for dangerous accumulation of ice on\ntheir vessel. If possible, remain in port, avoid the warning area\nor conduct mitigation.\n\nAnyone operating a smaller vessel should avoid navigating in\nhazardous conditions.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "MWWDLH"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [

                    ],

                "headline": "Small Craft Advisory issued January 18 at 3:16PM CST until January 20 at 12:00AM CST by NWS Duluth MN",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Small Craft Advisory, northwest winds 20 to 30\nkt and waves 5 to 8 ft expected. For the Heavy Freezing Spray\nWarning, heavy freezing spray at a rate of 2 cm per hour or\ngreater expected, and may rapidly accumulate on vessels.\n\n* WHERE...Duluth MN to Port Wing WI, Port Wing to Sand Island\nWI, Sand Island to Bayfield WI and Outer Apostle Islands\nBeyond 5 NM from Mainland.\n\n* WHEN...For the Small Craft Advisory, until midnight CST Monday\nnight. For the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning, from 8 PM this\nevening to 9 AM CST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in heavy freezing spray is\nhazardous. Freezing spray may render mechanical and electronic\ncomponents inoperative. Rapid ice accretion on decks and\nsuperstructures may result in a catastrophic loss of\nstability. Conditions will be hazardous to small craft.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Brief and localized gales up to 35 knots\nwill be possible late this evening and tonight.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for dangerous accumulation of ice on\ntheir vessel. If possible, remain in port, avoid the warning area\nor conduct mitigation.\n\nAnyone operating a smaller vessel should avoid navigating in\nhazardous conditions.",

                "headline": "Gale Warning issued January 18 at 2:51PM CST until January 19 at 3:00PM CST by NWS Chicago IL",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Gale Warning, northwest gales to 40 kt and\nsignificant waves to 12 ft occasionally to 16 ft expected. For\nthe Heavy Freezing Spray Warning, heavy freezing spray at a rate\nof 2 cm per hour or greater expected, and may rapidly\naccumulate on vessels.\n\n* WHERE...Gary to Michigan City IN.\n\n* WHEN...For the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning, from midnight\ntonight to 9 PM CST Monday. Freezing spray may render mechanical and electronic\ncomponents inoperative. Rapid ice accretion on decks and\nsuperstructures may result in a catastrophic loss of\nstability.                 "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for dangerous accumulation of ice on\ntheir vessel.                 "headline": "Cold Weather Advisory issued January 18 at 2:48PM CST until January 19 at 12:00PM CST by NWS Green Bay WI",
                "description": "* WHAT...Very cold wind chills as low as 30 below expected.\n\n* WHERE...Marathon, Portage, Wood, Langlade, Lincoln, Oneida, Vilas,\nand Forest Counties.\n\n* WHEN...From 3 AM to noon CST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The dangerously cold wind chills as low as 30 below zero\ncould cause frostbite on exposed skin in as little as 10 minutes.",

                "senderName": "NWS Buffalo NY",
                "headline": "Small Craft Advisory issued January 18 at 3:48PM EST until January 19 at 7:00AM EST by NWS Buffalo NY",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Small Craft Advisory, southwest winds 15 to 20\nknots. For the Gale Warning, west winds to 40 knots and waves 10\nto 14 feet expected. For the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning,\nheavy freezing spray at a rate of 2 cm per hour or greater\nexpected, and may rapidly accumulate on vessels.\n\n* WHERE...The nearshore waters of Lake Ontario from the Niagara\nRiver to Hamlin Beach.\n\n* WHEN...For the Small Craft Advisory, until 7 AM EST Monday.\nFor the Gale Warning, from 7 AM Monday to 1 AM EST Wednesday.\nFor the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning, from 7 AM Monday to 1 AM\nEST Wednesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in heavy freezing spray is\nhazardous. Freezing spray may render mechanical and electronic\ncomponents inoperative. Rapid ice accretion on decks and\nsuperstructures may result in a catastrophic loss of\nstability. Strong winds will cause hazardous waves which could\ncapsize or damage vessels and reduce visibility.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for dangerous accumulation of ice on\ntheir vessel. If possible, remain in port, avoid the warning area\nor conduct mitigation.\n\nMariners should alter plans to avoid these hazardous conditions.\nRemain in port, seek safe harbor, alter course, and/or secure the\nvessel for hazardous conditions.",

                "headline": "Gale Warning issued January 18 at 3:48PM EST until January 21 at 1:00AM EST by NWS Buffalo NY",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Small Craft Advisory, southwest winds 15 to 20\nknots. For the Gale Warning, west winds to 40 knots and waves 10\nto 14 feet expected. For the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning,\nheavy freezing spray at a rate of 2 cm per hour or greater\nexpected, and may rapidly accumulate on vessels.\n\n* WHERE...The nearshore waters of Lake Ontario from the Niagara\nRiver to Hamlin Beach.\n\n* WHEN...For the Small Craft Advisory, until 7 AM EST Monday.\nFor the Gale Warning, from 7 AM Monday to 1 AM EST Wednesday.\nFor the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning, from 7 AM Monday to 1 AM\nEST Wednesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in heavy freezing spray is\nhazardous. Freezing spray may render mechanical and electronic\ncomponents inoperative. Rapid ice accretion on decks and\nsuperstructures may result in a catastrophic loss of\nstability. Strong winds will cause hazardous waves which could\ncapsize or damage vessels and reduce visibility.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for dangerous accumulation of ice on\ntheir vessel.                 "senderName": "NWS Buffalo NY",
                "headline": "Gale Warning issued January 18 at 3:48PM EST until January 21 at 1:00AM EST by NWS Buffalo NY",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Gale Warning, west winds to 40 knots and waves\n15 to 20 feet expected. For the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning,\nheavy freezing spray at a rate of 2 cm per hour or greater\nexpected, and may rapidly accumulate on vessels.\n\n* WHERE...The open waters of Lake Ontario from the Niagara River\nto the Saint Lawrence River.\n\n* WHEN...From 7 AM Monday to 1 AM EST Wednesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in heavy freezing spray is\nhazardous. Freezing spray may render mechanical and electronic\ncomponents inoperative. Rapid ice accretion on decks and\nsuperstructures may result in a catastrophic loss of\nstability. Strong winds will cause hazardous waves which could\ncapsize or damage vessels and reduce visibility.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for dangerous accumulation of ice on\ntheir vessel. If possible, remain in port, avoid the warning area\nor conduct mitigation.\n\nMariners should alter plans to avoid these hazardous conditions.\nRemain in port, seek safe harbor, alter course, and/or secure the\nvessel for hazardous conditions.",

                "headline": "Gale Warning issued January 18 at 3:41PM EST until January 19 at 7:00PM EST by NWS Cleveland OH",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Low Water Advisory, abnormally low water levels\nexpected. For the Gale Warning, west winds 20 to 30 knots with\ngusts up to 40 knots and waves 1 to 4 feet expected.\n\n* WHERE...The nearshore waters of Lake Erie from Maumee Bay to\nVermilion OH, and the open waters of Lake Erie from Maumee Bay\nto Vermilion OH.\n\n* WHEN...For the Low Water Advisory, from 7 AM to 10 PM EST\nMonday. For the Gale Warning, from 7 AM to 7 PM EST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Strong winds will cause hazardous waves which could\ncapsize or damage vessels and reduce visibility. Below normal\nwater levels will result in hazardous navigating conditions.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...At Toledo, the critical mark for safe\nnavigation is currently 9 inches below low water datum. Water\nlevels are expected to fall to near or below that mark Monday\nmorning, with lowest levels expected Monday afternoon before\nwater levels rise Monday night.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should alter plans to avoid these hazardous conditions.\nRemain in port, seek safe harbor, alter course, and/or secure the\nvessel for hazardous conditions.\n\nMariners should navigate shallow waters with extreme caution due\nto below normal water levels.",

                "description": "* WHAT...For the Gale Warning, west winds 30 to 40 knots with\ngusts up to 50 knots and waves 9 to 15 feet expected. For the\nHeavy Freezing Spray Warning, heavy freezing spray at a rate of\n2 cm per hour or greater expected, and may rapidly accumulate on\nvessels.\n\n* WHERE...The open waters of Lake Erie from Avon Point OH to\nRipley NY.\n\n* WHEN...From 7 AM Monday to 1 AM EST Tuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in heavy freezing spray is\nhazardous. Freezing spray may render mechanical and electronic\ncomponents inoperative. Rapid ice accretion on decks and\nsuperstructures may result in a catastrophic loss of\nstability. Strong winds will cause hazardous waves which could\ncapsize or damage vessels and reduce visibility.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for dangerous accumulation of ice on\ntheir vessel.                 "headline": "Gale Warning issued January 18 at 3:40PM EST until January 19 at 7:00PM EST by NWS Marquette MI",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning, heavy freezing\nspray at a rate of 2 cm per hour or greater expected, and may\nrapidly accumulate on vessels. For the Gale Warning, northwest\nwinds 25 to 35 kt with gusts up to 40 kt and waves 10 to 15 ft\nexpected.\n\n* WHERE...Marquette to Munising MI, Munising to Grand Marais MI\nand Lake Superior East of a line from Manitou Island to\nMarquette MI and West of a line from Grand Marais MI to the\nUS/Canadian Border Beyond 5NM from shore.\n\n* WHEN...For the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning, from 1 AM Monday\nto 10 PM EST Tuesday. For the Gale Warning, from 1 AM to 7 PM\nEST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in heavy freezing spray is\nhazardous. Freezing spray may render mechanical and electronic\ncomponents inoperative. Rapid ice accretion on decks and\nsuperstructures may result in a catastrophic loss of\nstability. Strong winds will cause hazardous waves which could\ncapsize or damage vessels and reduce visibility.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for dangerous accumulation of ice on\ntheir vessel.                 "headline": "Heavy Freezing Spray Warning issued January 18 at 3:40PM EST until January 20 at 10:00PM EST by NWS Marquette MI",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning, heavy freezing\nspray at a rate of 2 cm per hour or greater expected, and may\nrapidly accumulate on vessels. For the Gale Warning, northwest\nwinds 25 to 35 kt with gusts up to 40 kt and waves 10 to 15 ft\nexpected.\n\n* WHERE...Marquette to Munising MI, Munising to Grand Marais MI\nand Lake Superior East of a line from Manitou Island to\nMarquette MI and West of a line from Grand Marais MI to the\nUS/Canadian Border Beyond 5NM from shore.\n\n* WHEN...For the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning, from 1 AM Monday\nto 10 PM EST Tuesday.                 "description": "* WHAT...Very cold wind chills as low as 22 to 29 below zero\nexpected.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of east central, south central, and southeast\nWisconsin.\n\n* WHEN...From 3 AM to noon CST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The dangerously cold wind chills as low as 30 below zero\ncould cause frostbite on exposed skin in as little as 30 minutes.\nFrostbite and hypothermia will occur if unprotected skin is\nexposed to these temperatures.",

                "senderName": "NWS Medford OR",
                "headline": "Air Stagnation Advisory issued January 18 at 12:15PM PST until January 21 at 4:00PM PST by NWS Medford OR",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Freezing Fog Advisory within the Illinois and lower\nRogue Valley in Josephine County, visibility under 1/4 mile in\ndense freezing fog. For the Air Stagnation Advisory, decreased air\nquality will likely continue. This is due to an inversion and\nstagnant air conditions near the surface that will continue to\ntrap pollutants.\n\n* WHERE...For the Freezing Fog Advisory and Air Stagnation Advisory,\nwithin the Illinois and lower Rogue Valley in Josephine County.\nFor the Air Stagnation Advisory, valleys in Josephine County.\n\n* WHEN...For the Air Stagnation Advisory, until 4 PM PST Wednesday.\nFor the Freezing Fog Advisory, from 8 PM this evening to noon PST\nMonday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Low visibility could make driving conditions hazardous,\nand locally slick conditions are possible.\n\nPoor air quality may cause issues for people with respiratory\nproblems.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...A current Freezing Fog Advisory is going to\nremain expired, as temperatures in the Illinois Valley and around\nGrants Pass have risen above freezing. With cool temperatures\nexpected to return tonight into Monday morning, an Advisory is in\nplace to address those forecast conditions.",
                "instruction": "Persons with respiratory illness should follow their physicians\nadvice for dealing with high levels of air pollution during periods\nof stagnant air.\n\nIf driving, slow down, use your headlights, and leave plenty of\ndistance ahead of you.",

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Milwaukee/Sullivan WI",
                "headline": "Gale Warning issued January 18 at 2:04PM CST until January 19 at 6:00PM CST by NWS Milwaukee/Sullivan WI",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Gale Warning, northwest winds 25 to 35 kt with\ngusts up to 40 kt and waves 8 to 13 ft expected. For the Heavy\nFreezing Spray Warning, heavy freezing spray at a rate of 2 cm\nper hour or greater expected, and may rapidly accumulate on\nvessels.\n\n* WHERE...All Open Waters of Lake Michigan.\n\n* WHEN...For the Gale Warning, from midnight tonight to 6 PM CST\nMonday. For the Heavy Freezing Spray Warning, from midnight\ntonight to noon CST Tuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in heavy freezing spray is\nhazardous. Freezing spray may render mechanical and electronic\ncomponents inoperative. Rapid ice accretion on decks and\nsuperstructures may result in a catastrophic loss of\nstability. Strong winds will cause hazardous waves which could\ncapsize or damage vessels and reduce visibility.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for dangerous accumulation of ice on\ntheir vessel. If possible, remain in port, avoid the warning area\nor conduct mitigation.\n\nMariners should alter plans to avoid these hazardous conditions.\nRemain in port, seek safe harbor, alter course, and/or secure the\nvessel for severe conditions.",
                "response": "Avoid",

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Indianapolis IN",
                "headline": "Special Weather Statement issued January 18 at 2:58PM EST by NWS Indianapolis IN",
                "description": "A 3 to 4 hour period of light snow is expected this evening into\nthe early overnight with scattered flurries lingering through the\nremainder of the night. Light accumulations less than an inch are\npossible focused especially across the northern half of central\nIndiana. Untreated roads will become slick and snow covered.\nIncreasing winds and bitterly cold temperatures late tonight into\nMonday morning will support blowing snow, especially in rural\nareas.\n\nChallenging travel conditions are possible this evening through\nMonday morning. Use caution and allow for extra time to reach\nyour destination.",

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Portland OR",
                "headline": "Air Stagnation Advisory issued January 18 at 11:55AM PST until January 21 at 4:00PM PST by NWS Portland OR",
                "description": "* WHAT...Light winds and limited air movement will lead to a period\nof stagnant air conditions.\n\n* WHERE...Central and Southern Willamette Valley, and Tualatin\nValley.\n\n* WHEN...Until 4 PM PST Wednesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Deteriorating air quality may cause issues for people\nwith respiratory problems.",
                "instruction": "State air quality agencies highly recommend that no outdoor burning\noccur and that residential wood burning devices be limited as much\nas possible. According to state air quality agencies, prolonged\nperiods of stagnant air can hold pollutants close to the ground\nwhere people live and breathe. Check with your local burn agency for\nany current restrictions in your area.\n\nPeople with respiratory illness should follow their physician's\nadvice for dealing with high levels of air pollution during periods\nof stagnant air, especially if an Air Quality Alert is in effect for\nyour area. To see if an Air Quality Alert is in effect for your\narea, go to www.weather.gov/pqr and left-click your location on the\nhomepage map display. Alternatively, you could also type your\nlocation into the search bar found on the top-left hand side of the\nhomepage. From there, you will see an Air Quality Alert listed at\nthe top if one is in effect for your location; you can click the\nalert to read more details if applicable.",
                "response": "Execute",

                    ],

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Medford OR",
                "headline": "Beach Hazards Statement issued January 18 at 11:52AM PST until January 20 at 1:00PM PST by NWS Medford OR",
                "description": "* WHAT...Moderate risk of sneaker waves expected.\n\n* WHERE...Beaches of Douglas, Coos and Curry Counties.\n\n* WHEN...From late Monday evening through early Tuesday\nafternoon.\n\n* IMPACTS...Sneaker waves can run up significantly farther on\nbeaches than normal, including over rocks and jetties. These\nwaves can suddenly knock people off of their feet and sweep\nthem into the ocean. The waves can also move logs or other\nobjects which could crush or trap anyone caught underneath.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...While sneaker waves can occur at any time,\nthe greatest risk is on an incoming tide. Please be aware of the\ntides if venturing out onto the beaches. The high tide Tuesday\non southern Oregon beaches is roughly 11 am to 1 pm PST.\n\n* View the hazard area in detail at\nhttps://www.wrh.noaa.gov/map/?wfo=mfr",
                "instruction": "Avoid climbing on rocks and jetties. Stay away from the ocean and\nremain out of the water to avoid hazardous conditions.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {

                        "CFWMFR"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [
                        "WHUS46 KMFR 181952"

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Northern Indiana",
                "headline": "Small Craft Advisory issued January 18 at 2:49PM EST until January 19 at 1:00AM EST by NWS Northern Indiana",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Small Craft Advisory, waves 2 to 5 feet. For\nthe Gale Warning, west winds 20 to 30 knots with gusts up to\n35 knots and waves 7 to 12 feet expected. For the Heavy\nFreezing Spray Warning, heavy freezing spray at a rate of 2 cm\nper hour or greater expected, and may rapidly accumulate on\nvessels.\n\n* WHERE...Michigan City IN to St Joseph MI.\n\n* WHEN...For the Small Craft Advisory, until 1 AM EST Monday.\nFor the Gale Warning, from 1 AM to 4 PM EST Monday. For the\nHeavy Freezing Spray Warning, from 1 AM Monday to 7 AM EST\nTuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Operating a vessel in heavy freezing spray is\nhazardous. Freezing spray may render mechanical and electronic\ncomponents inoperative. Rapid ice accretion on decks and\nsuperstructures may result in a catastrophic loss of\nstability. Strong winds will cause hazardous waves which could\ncapsize or damage vessels and reduce visibility.",
                "instruction": "Mariners should prepare for dangerous accumulation of ice on\ntheir vessel. If possible, remain in port, avoid the warning area\nor conduct mitigation.\n\nMariners should alter plans to avoid these hazardous conditions.\nRemain in port, seek safe harbor, alter course, and/or secure the\nvessel for hazardous conditions.",
                "response": "Avoid",

                    ],

                "senderName": "NWS Detroit/Pontiac MI",
                "headline": "Winter Weather Advisory issued January 18 at 2:45PM EST until January 20 at 10:00AM EST by NWS Detroit/Pontiac MI",
                "description": "* WHAT...Snow and blowing snow expected. Total snow accumulations 1\nto 3 inches, with locally higher amounts possible. Winds gusting\n40 to 45 MPH. Wind chills Monday Night night and Tuesday Morning 5\nto 15 degrees below zero.\n\n* WHERE...All of Southeast Michigan\n\n* WHEN...From midnight tonight to 10 AM EST Tuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Plan on slippery road conditions and poor visibilities.\nThe hazardous conditions could impact the Monday morning and\nevening commutes. Gusty winds may cause localized power outages.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Brief snow squalls are likely, leading to\nnear white out conditions at times. Snow showers and squalls will\nbe more frequent along and south of the I-69 corridor and along\nand north of the I-94 corridor.",
                "instruction": "Slow down and use caution while traveling. You can obtain the latest\nroad conditions from the MDOT Michigan Drive Map.\n\nPersons are urged to stay indoors until conditions improve. If you\nmust go outside, dress in layers. Several layers of clothes will\nkeep you warmer than a single heavy coat. Cover exposed skin to\nreduce your risk of frostbite or hypothermia. Gloves, a scarf, and a\nhat will keep you from losing your body heat.",

                "headline": "Cold Weather Advisory issued January 18 at 1:36PM CST until January 19 at 12:00PM CST by NWS Lincoln IL",
                "description": "* WHAT...Very cold wind chills as low as 10 to 20 below expected\nlate tonight and Monday morning.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of central, east central, and west central\nIllinois.\n\n* WHEN...From 3 AM to noon CST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The dangerously cold wind chills as low as 20 below zero\ncould cause frostbite on exposed skin in as little as 30 minutes.",
                "instruction": "Use caution while traveling outside. Wear appropriate clothing, a\nhat, and gloves.\n\nKeep pets indoors as much as possible.\n\nMake frequent checks on older family, friends, and neighbors. Ensure\nportable heaters are used correctly. Do not use generators or grills\ninside.\n\nIf you do not have heat in your home or lack shelter, you can call\n211 or visit 211illinois.org for assistance locating appropriate\nshelter from the cold.",

                "senderName": "NWS Northern Indiana",
                "headline": "Winter Weather Advisory issued January 18 at 2:35PM EST until January 20 at 1:00AM EST by NWS Northern Indiana",
                "description": "* WHAT...Lake effect snow and blowing snow expected. Total snow\naccumulations between 3 and 6 inches. Winds gusting as high as 40\nmph. Blowing and drifting snow. Visibilities likely reduced to\nless than one quarter of a mile at times due to the combination of\nsnow and blowing snow.\n\n* WHERE...Cass MI, Northern Berrien, and Southern Berrien Counties.\n\n* WHEN...From 7 PM this evening to 1 AM EST Tuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Roads, and especially bridges and overpasses, will\nlikely become slick and hazardous. Travel could be very difficult.\nThe hazardous conditions could impact the Monday morning and\nevening commutes.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Low visibility will be possible from falling\nsnow and gusty winds. Use caution will driving and increase your\nfollowing distance. Cold temperatures combined with the gusty\nwinds will produce wind chills of around 10 below on Monday. Be\nsure to bundle up if outside.",
                "instruction": "Slow down and use caution while traveling. The latest road\nconditions for the state you are calling from can be obtained by\ncalling 5 1 1.",

                "headline": "Winter Weather Advisory issued January 18 at 2:35PM EST until January 19 at 7:00PM EST by NWS Northern Indiana",
                "description": "* WHAT...Lake effect snow and blowing snow expected. Total snow\naccumulations ranging from 2 to 4 inches north of the Toll Road,\nto 1 to 2 inches south of the Toll Road. Blowing and drifting\nsnow, especially late tonight through mid afternoon Monday.\nVisibilities of less than one quarter of a mile due to snow and\nblowing snow are possible at times, with the greatest impacts\nexpected along and north of the Toll Road. Winds gusting as high\nas 35 mph.\n\n* WHERE...Eastern St. Joseph IN, Elkhart, Northern La Porte, and\nWestern St. Joseph IN Counties.\n\n* WHEN...From 7 PM EST /6 PM CST/ this evening to 7 PM EST /6 PM\nCST/ Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Roads, and especially bridges and overpasses, will\nlikely become slick and hazardous. Plan on slippery road\nconditions. The hazardous conditions could impact the Monday\nmorning commute.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Low visibility will be possible from falling\nsnow and gusty winds. Use caution will driving and increase your\nfollowing distance. Cold temperatures combined with the gusty\nwinds will produce wind chills of 10 below to 15 below on Monday.\nBe sure to bundle up if outside.",
                "instruction": "Slow down and use caution while traveling. The latest road\nconditions for the state you are calling from can be obtained by\ncalling 5 1 1.",

                "description": "* WHAT...Stagnant conditions will have potential to trap pollutants\nnear the ground with deteriorated air quality.\n\n* WHERE...Number 1 Canyon, Colville, Ione, Pangborn Airport,\nSpringdale-Hunters Road, Cashmere, Disautel Pass, Highway 20\nWauconda Summit, Conconully, Twisp, Entiat, Okanogan, Boulder\nCreek Road, Number 2 Canyon, Leavenworth, Chelan, Deer Park,\nMetaline, Wenatchee, Brewster, Chewelah, Omak, Oroville, Methow,\nWinthrop, Wauconda, Newport, Kettle Falls, Loup Loup Pass, Plain,\nStehekin, Northport, Stevens Pass, Nespelem, Holden Village,\nFruitland, Mazama, Sherman Pass, Metaline Falls, Bridgeport,\nFlowery Trail Road, Tiger, Republic, Chesaw Road, Orin-Rice Road,\nInchelium, and Tonasket.\n\n* WHEN...Until 4 PM PST Tuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Periods of air stagnation can lead to the buildup of\npollutants near the surface.",

                "headline": "Winter Storm Warning issued January 18 at 12:49PM EST until January 21 at 4:00PM EST by NWS Buffalo NY",
                "description": "* WHAT...Heavy snow and blowing snow expected. Total snow\naccumulations 10 to 20 inches. Winds gusting as high as 50 mph\ncreating blowing and drifting snow and at times white out\nconditions.\n\n* WHERE...Niagara, Orleans, Northern Erie, and Genesee Counties.\nGreatest snow accumulation will be found across southern Niagara,\nsouthwestern Orleans, northern Erie and western Genesee counties.\n\n* WHEN...Now to 4 PM EST Wednesday. Significant snow accumulation\nwill occur late tonight through Monday, and again Tuesday into\nTuesday night.\n\n* IMPACTS...Whiteout conditions are expected and will make travel\ntreacherous and potentially life-threatening. Travel will be very\ndifficult in deep snow covered roads. The hazardous conditions\nwill impact the Monday morning through the Wednesday morning\ncommutes. Gusty winds will down tree branches and cause isolated\npower outages.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Expect snowband oscillate with varying\nintensities and snowfall amounts over the area. Snowfall rates 2\ninches per hour late tonight through Monday, and again Tuesday\nthrough Tuesday evening. Wind chill values zero to 10 below zero\nMonday night through Tuesday night.",

                "instruction": "If you must travel, keep an extra flashlight, food, and water in\nyour vehicle in case of an emergency.\n\nDuring lake effect snow, the weather can vary from bands of locally\nheavy snow to dry weather just a few miles away.                 "headline": "Freeze Warning issued January 18 at 10:29AM EST until January 19 at 9:00AM EST by NWS Jacksonville FL",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Freeze Warning, sub-freezing temperatures as low as\n31 expected. For the Cold Weather Advisory, very cold wind chills\nas low as 24 expected.\n\n* WHERE...A portion of northeast Florida.\n\n* WHEN...For the Freeze Warning, from 9 PM this evening to 9 AM EST\nMonday. For the Cold Weather Advisory, from 1 AM to 9 AM EST\nMonday.\n\n* IMPACTS...The cold wind chills as low as 25 degrees could result\nin hypothermia or frostbite if precautions are not taken. Wind\nchill values can lead to hypothermia with prolonged exposure.\nAppropriate action should be taken to ensure tender vegetation and\noutdoor pets have adequate protection from the cold temperatures.\nYoung children, the elderly and the homeless are especially\nvulnerable to the cold. Take measures to protect them.",
                "instruction": "Use caution while traveling outside. Wear appropriate clothing, a\nhat, and gloves.\n\nKeep pets indoors as much as possible.\n\nMake frequent checks on older family, friends, and neighbors. Ensure\nportable heaters are used correctly. Do not use generators or grills\ninside.\n\nIf you do not have heat in your home or lack shelter, you can call\n211 for assistance locating appropriate shelter from the cold.\n\nTake steps now to protect tender plants from the cold.",

                "headline": "Cold Weather Advisory issued January 18 at 10:29AM EST until January 19 at 9:00AM EST by NWS Jacksonville FL",
                "description": "* WHAT...For the Freeze Warning, sub-freezing temperatures as low as\n25 expected. For the Cold Weather Advisory, very cold wind chills\nas low as 22 expected. For the Freeze Watch, sub-freezing\ntemperatures as low as 28 possible.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of northeast and northern Florida.\n\n* WHEN...For the Freeze Warning, from 9 PM this evening to 9 AM EST\nMonday. For the Cold Weather Advisory, from 1 AM to 9 AM EST\nMonday. For the Freeze Watch, from late Monday night through\nTuesday morning.\n\n* IMPACTS...The cold wind chills as low as 20 degrees could result\nin hypothermia or frostbite if precautions are not taken. Wind\nchill values can lead to hypothermia with prolonged exposure.\nAppropriate action should be taken to ensure tender vegetation and\noutdoor pets have adequate protection from the cold temperatures.\nYoung children, the elderly and the homeless are especially\nvulnerable to the cold. Take measures to protect them.",
                "instruction": "Use caution while traveling outside.                 "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Anchorage AK",
                "headline": "Special Weather Statement issued January 18 at 6:05AM AKST by NWS Anchorage AK",
                "description": "The Chugach NF Avalanche Information Center in Girdwood has\nissued a SPECIAL AVALANCHE STATEMENT for the following areas:\nSouth Fork Eagle River, Chugach State Park, Girdwood, Portage,\nTurnagain Pass, Moose Pass, Summit Lake, and Seward from 6 am\nSaturday, Jan 17 to 6 am Tuesday, Jan 20.\n\n* WHAT...Very dangerous avalanche conditions exist in the\nbackcountry. Avalanche danger is HIGH at and above treeline.\n\n* WHERE...For the Western Chugach and Kenai Mountains in and\naround Anchorage, Girdwood, Portage, Turnagain Pass, Summit\nLake, Moose Pass, Lost Lake, and Seward.\n\n* WHEN...In effect through 6:00 am Tuesday, Jan 20th.\n\n* IMPACTS...Very large human-triggered avalanches are likely.\nDespite quiet weather, the snowpack remains very unstable.\nPeople can easily trigger very large avalanches. It is likely\navalanches may be triggered remotely from flat terrain below\nsteeper slopes.\n\n* PRECAUTIONARY / PREPAREDNESS ACTIONS... If deciding to recreate\nin the backcountry, choose terrain that is flat or less than 30\ndegrees in steepness, with nothing steeper above you. Travel is\navalanche terrain is not recommended.\n\nSimilar avalanche danger may exist at locations outside the\ncoverage area of this or any avalanche center.\n\nFor more information visit www.ChugachAvalanche.org.",
                "instruction": null,
                "response": "Execute",

                "headline": "Rip Current Statement issued January 18 at 7:35AM EST until January 19 at 11:00AM EST by NWS Tampa Bay Ruskin FL",
                "description": "* WHAT...Dangerous rip currents expected.\n\n* WHERE...Pinellas, Coastal Hillsborough, Coastal Manatee,\nCoastal Sarasota, Coastal Charlotte and Coastal Lee Counties.\n\n* WHEN...From 11 AM EST this morning through Monday morning.\n\n* IMPACTS...Rip currents can sweep even the best swimmers away\nfrom shore into deeper water.",
                "instruction": "Swim near a lifeguard. If caught in a rip current, relax and\nfloat. Don't swim against the current. If able, swim in a\ndirection following the shoreline. If unable to escape, face the\nshore and call or wave for help.",

                "event": "Special Weather Statement",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Tiyan GU",
                "headline": "Special Weather Statement issued January 18 at 5:19PM ChST by NWS Tiyan GU",
                "description": "A broad tropical disturbance continues to produce unsettled weather\nacross much of Chuuk State. This disturbance remains poorly\norganized, with an elongated circulation lingering near Nukuoro in\nsouthern Pohnpei State and broad trough extending northwest, passing\nwest of Chuuk Lagoon. As this disturbance slowly reorganizes, it is\nexpected to maintain a period of unsettled weather through Monday\nmorning. Numerous locally heavy showers and isolated thunderstorms\nare expected. Recent observations show strong gusts becoming less\nfrequent since last night, however occasional gusts to 25 kt remain\npossible near heavier showers. Around 3 to 5 inches of rain has\nfallen since Friday and an additional 1 to 2 inches are possible\nthrough Monday. Once this disturbance moves west of Chuuk, scattered\nto numerous showers and isolated thunderstorms within the trailing\ntrade-wind convergence may prolong showery weather through midweek,\nbut the threat of heavy rainfall, gusty winds and choppy seas\nsubsides. Further updates will be provided as we monitor trends in\nsatellite observations and model guidance.\n\nResidents and mariners should continue monitor the latest forecasts\nfor the region at weather.gov/gum/PublicForecasts and any information\nshared by WSO Chuuk for their local region.",
                "instruction": null,
                "response": "Execute",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "SPSPQ2"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [
                        "WWPQ82 PGUM 180719"
                    ],
                    "NWSheadline": [
                        "HEAVY RAINFALL, GUSTY WINDS AND CHOPPY SEAS CONTINUE THROUGH MONDAY MORNING AS A TROPICAL DISTURBANCE MOVES THROUGH CHUUK STATE"

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Medford OR",
                "headline": "Air Quality Alert issued January 17 at 5:41PM PST by NWS Medford OR",
                "description": "Oregon Department of Environmental Quality has issued an Air\nPollution Advisory...in effect until noon PST Tuesday.\n\nWildfires burning in the region combined with forecast conditions\nwill cause air quality levels to fluctuate and could be at unhealthy\nlevels.\n\nSmoke levels can change rapidly depending on the weather. People at\nrisk include infants and young children, people with heart or lung\ndisease, older adults and pregnant people.\n\nPeople can take the following precautions to protect their health:\n\n- Follow local burn restrictions to prevent deteriorating air\nquality.\n- Avoid strenuous outdoor activity during periods of poor air quality.\n- People with heart or lung problems and young children are\nespecially vulnerable. These people should stay indoors while smoke\nlevels are high.\n- Use certified High Efficiency Particulate Air (HEPA) filters in\nindoor heating, ventilation, cooling and air purification systems.\n- Avoid using wood-burning stoves and other sources of indoor smoke\nif possible.\n\nFor additional information...please visit the web site at\nhttps://oregonsmoke.org",
                "instruction": null,
                "response": "Monitor",

                "senderName": "NWS Pendleton OR",
                "headline": "Air Quality Alert issued January 16 at 11:31AM PST by NWS Pendleton OR",
                "description": "Oregon Department of Environmental Quality has issued an Air Quality\nAdvisory until 10 AM PST Tuesday.\n\nFor the Air Quality Advisory, stagnant air conditions will trap\nsmoke and other air pollutants near the ground where people breathe.\n\nCheck with your local health or air agency for current restrictions\non woodstove use. There are often exceptions for those who use wood\nexclusively to heat their homes and those with limited income.\n\nPeople can take the following precautions to protect their health:\n\n* Follow local burn restrictions to prevent deteriorating air\nquality.\n* Avoid strenuous outdoor activity in smoky conditions.\n* People with heart or lung problems and young children are\nespecially vulnerable. These people should stay indoors while\nsmoke levels are high.\n* Use certified High Efficiency Particulate Air (HEPA) filters in\nindoor heating ventilation, cooling, and air purification systems.\n* Avoid using wood-burning stoves and other sources of indoor smoke\nif possible.\n\nFor additional information...please visit the Web site at\nhttp://www.oregon.gov/DEQ",
                "instruction": null,
                "response": "Monitor",

                "description": "* WHAT...Stagnation conditions will trap pollutants, leading to\ndeteriorating air quality, particularly in lower elevations and\nvalleys.\n\n* WHERE...Orofino/Grangeville Region, Southern Clearwater Mountains,\nand Northern Clearwater Mountains.\n\n* WHEN...Until 4 PM PST Tuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Periods of air stagnation can lead to the build up of\npollutants near the surface.",
                "instruction": "Persons with respiratory illness should follow their physicians\nadvice for dealing with high levels of air pollution during periods\nof stagnant air.\n\nAccording to state air quality agencies, prolonged period of\nstagnant air can hold pollutants close to the ground where people\nlive and breathe.
